coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric/electrochemical dual-channel sensors based on carbon quantum dots for the detection and information anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Rui
Mo, Zunli
Pei, Hebing
He, Simin
Zheng, Hui
Liu, Wanjing
Jin, Xuening
Zhang, Xuejiao
Liu, Nijuan
Guo, Ruibin
coredata.dc:description: Sensors used to detect Fe3+ and Cys are generally a single method. In this work, two methods of fluorescence and electrochemistry are innovatively combined to detect Fe3+ and Cys, and the obtained Lod is basically the same. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with blue fluorescence were successfully prepared by hydrothermal methods using tryptophan (Trp) as carbon source, which can be used for fluorescence and electrochemical dual-channel probe quickly detecting iron ion (Fe3+) and cysteine (Cys). A series of studies have been carried out on its morphology, chemical structure, and optical properties through TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis absorption spectrum, and photoluminescence spectra. And the quantum yield of CQDs was up to 52.64%. It was also analyzed in the real sample, demonstrating the feasibility and reliability of detecting Fe3+ and Cys in the real sample. Furthermore, CQDs can also be used as fluorescent ink for information anti-counterfeiting due to their excellent fluorescent properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Iron ion
Cysteine
Fluorescence detection
Electrochemical detection
Information anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and Cys
Fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Fe3+
Fluorescence recovery of CQDs/Fe3+ by Cys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM spectrogram of CQDs, illustration is particle size statistics for CQDs; (b) Magnified image of TEM spectrum.
(a) X-ray powder diffraction spectrum of CQDs; (b) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs and Trp; (c) XPS survey spectrum of CQDs; High resolution XPS spectrum of (d) C1s; (e) N1s; (f) O1s.

(a) Left: UV–vis spectra of CQDs; Right: excitation and emission spectra of CQDs (The illustration is a physical drawing under fluorescent and ultraviolet lamps); (b) Emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 340 to 400 nm; (c) Stability test of CQDs probe; (d) Fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence of increasing NaCl concentrations from 0 to 1 M.

(a) The fluorescence intensity of CQDs is induced by different types of metal iron: Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Li+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of CQDs, F is the fluorescence intensity after the metal ions are added; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of increasing Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 400 μM; (c) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentrations from 1 to 10 μM; (d) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentrations from 10 to 100 μM.

(a) The fluorescence intensity of CQDs is induced by different types of amino acids: Cys, Tar, Met, Lys, Ser, Pro, His, Tyr, La, Phe, Man, Trp, Glu, blank is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs + Fe3+; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of increasing Cys concentrations from 0 to 200 μM; (c) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Cys concentrations from 3 to 10 μM; (d) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Cys concentrations from 10 to 50 μM.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs and the CQDs solution containing Fe3+; (b) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ (red line) and excitation (brown line) and emission (blue line) spectrum of CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) CQDs detect electrochemical test diagrams for Cys (0–200 μM); (b) The relationship between current and Cys concentrations from 10 to 100 μM.
Schematic diagram of preparation and detection strategy of CQDs based probe.

head.ce:title: Fluorometric/electrochemical dual-channel sensors based on carbon quantum dots for the detection and information anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials, Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials
Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials
Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials, Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensors used to detect Fe3+ and Cys are generally a single method. In this work, two methods of fluorescence and electrochemistry are innovatively combined to detect Fe3+ and Cys, and the obtained Lod is basically the same. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with blue fluorescence were successfully prepared by hydrothermal methods using tryptophan (Trp) as carbon source, which can be used for fluorescence and electrochemical dual-channel probe quickly detecting iron ion (Fe3+) and cysteine (Cys). A series of studies have been carried out on its morphology, chemical structure, and optical properties through TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis absorption spectrum, and photoluminescence spectra. And the quantum yield of CQDs was up to 52.64%. It was also analyzed in the real sample, demonstrating the feasibility and reliability of detecting Fe3+ and Cys in the real sample. Furthermore, CQDs can also be used as fluorescent ink for information anti-counterfeiting due to their excellent fluorescent properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: All carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterials (all three dimensions are within the 1–100 nm range) can be called carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which is different from the materials in the carbon family in the past, the size is usually at the nanoscale, with excellent optical properties, mainly because CQDs have the quantum limit and boundary effects, in addition to good biocompatibility, and fluorescence strength. CQDs probes play an inestimable role in detecting agricultural drug residues [1], pesticides [2], food additives [3], ions [4] and amino acids [5].

Common quantum dots are divided into two categories: those based on heavy metals such as CdS QDs and CdTe QDs, which are limited by their toxicity [6,7]. Raw materials for the preparation of Carbon-based CQDs are particularly widely sourced, according to published articles have been crayfish shell [8], onions [9], honey [10], cucumbers [11], soot [12], garbage [13], etc. CQDs have good electrical conductivity and stand out in terms of fluorescence. Carbon-based CQDs are generally nontoxic compared to heavy metal QDs. Yan et al. cultured carbon-based CQDs with cells, and cell apoptosis did not exceed 10%, proving that carbon-based CQDs are basically nontoxic [14].

Iron is a trace element necessary for the human body, in the body metabolism can be repeatedly used by the body, there is almost no other way to lose it. Erika P. Raven's group indicated that iron levels are associated with Alzheimer's disease, so rapid and low-toxic testing is very necessary [15]. Cysteine (Cys), is the only amino acid with reducing group -SH in more than 20 amino acids that make up proteins and is widely used in beauty, food, and medicine. Cys is also an amino acid antidote, which is involved in the cell reduction process and phospholipid metabolism in the liver, has to protect liver cells from damage, promotes liver function recovery and exuberant pharmacological effects [16].

Methods commonly used to detect Fe3+ and Cys are include gas chromatography [17], liquid chromatography [18], electrochemical sensing signaling [19], colorimetric [20,21], and fluorescent probe [22]. Among them, gas or liquid chromatography can get very accurate results, but usually have higher requirements for samples, time-consuming and laborious. Therefore, the characteristics of the fluorescent probe method and electrochemical sensing signaling are highlighted, the signal output method is diverse, has high sensitivity, and can real-time analysis.

Borah's group have developed l-cysteine modified caffeic acid mediated gold nanoparticles (Cys-Caf-AuNP) that showed promising application in detection of Fe3+. They were used to selectively detect Fe3+ in the 0.5–5 µM range with an LOD of 0.22 µM [23]. A water-soluble decaamine derivative of pillar [5]arene (APA) bearing triazole and amide moieties is synthesized by Joseph. The APA selectively detected Fe3+ (Lod = 689 ppm). The FeAPA selectively detects Cys (Lod = 1740 ppm) and detected F− (Lod = 434 ppm) [24]. Fluorescent probes already had significant advantages in detecting Fe3+ and Cys, and have extremely low Lod. In addition to fluorescence detection, we innovatively combined electrochemical detection with it to construct a dual-channel probe for Fe3+ and Cys detection, and the obtained Lod is basically the same.

Herein, we use Trp as the carbon source, through one step of hydrothermal methods small molecule thermal cracking polymerization into CQDs in Scheme 1 , when adding Fe3+ to CQDs, and then Fe3+ and CQDs combine to produce non-luminescent complexes to cause fluorescence to annihilate, while other ions do not, further indicating that the CQDs fluorescence probe has a high selectivity for Fe3+. When adding Cys to CQDs/Fe3+, fluorescence is restored. Electrochemical testing is also used, and the LOD is the same as the fluorescence. Furthermore, the CQDs can be used as fluorescent ink for information anti-counterfeiting.

Amino acids including: Methionine (Met), Histidine (His), and Serine (Ser) were purchased from Macklin Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tartaric acid (Tar), Glutamic acid (Glu), Proline (Pro), Mandelic acid (Man), Tyrosine (Tyr), and Lactic acid (La) were purchased from Aladdin Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phenylalanine (Phe) was purchased from Energy Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cysteine (Cys), Lysine (Lys), and Tryptophan (Trp) were purchased from Yuan ye Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O, LiCl, CdCl2·2.5H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, CoCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, PbC4H6O4·3H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained by JEM-3010 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray powder diffraction spectrum (XRD) was recorded on the D8 ADVANCE diffraction instrument (Bruker AXS, Germany). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum was performed on FTS-3000 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron energy spectrum (XPS) spectrum was carried out with Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo, USA). The photoluminescence spectrums were recorded on the FluoroMax-4 Fluorescence Spectrometer (HORIBA, USA). Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) images were obtained by the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China).

In this study, we use Trp as the carbon source, by one step of hydrothermal methods small molecules thermal cracking polymerization into CQDs. The specific method is: 1.02 g Trp dissolved into 70 mL of deionized water, stirred well. Next, the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 2.5 h. Subsequently, the reactor was cooled to room temperature, and filtered with a membrane of 0.22 μm to remove large particles. It was steamed for 24 h with a dialysis bag of 500–1000 Da. Finally, freeze and dry into a light-yellow fluffy powder.

Fluorescent quantum yield refers to the utilization of photons in photochemical reactions and can be defined as the number of molecules produced by each quantum absorbed. There are two commonly used methods to determine QY, one is the relative method [25,26], and the other is the absolute method [27–30]. The measurement of the relative method must rely on reference objects, which limits its applicability. In this experiment, the QY of CQDs was determined by FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. Fig. S1 shows the spectra of blank and CQDs. First, the excitation corrected intensity of blank and CQDs were measured by integrating spheres in Fig. S1(a). The absorption photon number P1 was evaluated from the area obtained by subtracting the excitation light peak of CQDs from background of blank. And then the emission corrected intensity of blank and CQDs were measured by integrating spheres in Fig. S1(b). The emission photon number P2 was evaluated from the area obtained by subtracting the background of blank from the emission peak of CQDs. The absolute QY was calculated from the ratio of P2/P1, and the QY of the CQDs was 52.64%. This measurement method has the advantages of being simple and fast, good repeat stability and small error.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration range of ions and amino acids was 0–400 μM and 0–200 μM respectively, with 2 mL 1 μg/mL of CQDs as the main solution, and after adding 30 μL different concentrations of ions and amino acids mixed 5 min, the test was carried out at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

Saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode, 1 mg/mL of CQDs solution, 8 uL modified glass carbon electrode as the working electrode, under the three-electrode system to test the cyclic voltammetry curve (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), electrochemical detection by the change of peak current intensity. The detection solution was tested using 10 mL 5 mM FeCl3·6H2O, followed by the addition of different concentrations of Cys, the volume added was 300 μL, and the working electrode and FeCl3·6H2O solution needed to be replaced again each time the test.

The urine samples were subjected to a 50-fold dilution to reduce the inference from urine samples and spiked with different concentrations of Cys added [31]. The lake water sample was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The concentration was calculated according to the standard curve and compared with the actual concentration to calculate the fortified recoveries. Each set of experiments was tested three times.

To completely determine the structural features of CQDs, we characterized them by TEM, XRD, FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS. The morphology and microstructure of CQDs were observed by TEM and HRTEM. The preparation of CQDs is characterized by TEM in Fig. 1 (a), which can be seen as spherical, uniform dispersion not reuniting, and the average particle size is 2.7 ± 0.2 nm. The lattice spacing of CQDs was 0.21 nm in Fig. 1(b), corresponding to the (1 0 0) plane of graphite, which indicates that the CQDs have a graphite-like structure core [32,33]. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum in Fig. 2 (a) was used to examine the crystallinity/graphitization of CQDs. The XRD patterns exhibit peaks at around 23.8°and 26.1°(2θ), which is indexed to the (1 0 0) planes of carbon and indicates the CQDs have a graphite-like structure [34,35]. XRD spectrum of CQDs shows diffraction peaks at 17.2–19.3°(2θ), which is contributed by the (0 0 2) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials [36–38].

The chemical composition of CQDs can be characterized by FT-IR and XPS. To further characterize what kinds of functional groups the surface of CQDs has, the FT-IR spectra of raw material Trp and product CQDs are compared. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the peak at 3402 cm−1 is attributed to –OH/–NH/–NH2 vibration, compared with raw material Trp, this band becomes wider, which may be induced by the association among hydrogen bonds. Peaks at 1665 cm−1 are telescopic vibrations of C-O; bands at 1386 cm−1 correspond to asymmetric and symmetrical telescopic vibrations at COO–. The bands at 1232 cm−1 and 1095 cm−1 represent the retractable vibrations of –CONH and C-O, the –CONH peak suggests that Trp forms CQDs through intermolecular or intramolecular dehydration condensation during the hydrothermal methods [39]. It is clear from the above data that CQDs have oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface.

The XPS survey spectrum of CQDs is depicted in Fig. 2(c), the characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s at 284.08 eV, 400.08 eV and 531.08 eV, respectively indicate that CQDs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen [40]. And the test data show that carbon accounts for 71.40% and nitrogen for 12.72%, oxygen accounted for 15.88%. The atomic percentages of these elements further testify to the presence and proportion of the elements. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the main peak of C1s present four peaks, including 284.28 eV (C-C/C=C), 285.08 eV (C–N), 286.38 eV (C–O) and 288.48 eV (C=O)[41]; N1s present three peaks, including 399.36 eV (Pyrrolic N), 400.58 eV (N–C) and 401.38 eV (amino N) in Fig. 2(e) [39]; O1s present three peaks, including 530.68 eV (O-C), 531.48 eV (O=C) and 532.38 eV (HO-C/C-O-C) in Fig. 2(f) [42]. These results show that the prepared CQDs surface has oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups, this indicates that the as-prepared CQDs are Trp surface-modified CQDs. And these results are the same as those of FT-IR.

The optical properties, including absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the as-prepared CQDs were evaluated. It is clear from the UV–vis spectra of CQDs that there are two distinct absorption peaks at 218 nm and 280 nm in Fig. 3 (a), with a tail extending into the visible range. The strong absorption peak at 218 nm is attributed to the π–π* transition of C = C. The UV–vis spectra of CQDs showed a weak peak at 280 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of C = O [43,44].

As shown in Fig. 3(b), it can be seen that with the excitation wavelength from 340 to 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity increases first and then decreases, the maximum value appears under the excitation condition of 370 nm, and the emission wavelength position does not shift, indicating that its emission characteristics are independent of excitation, this fluorescence property, which is independent of excitation, may depend on the surface state of the GQDs, rather than the topography [45–51]. The maximum excitation wavelength was obtained in the range of 340 nm-400 nm, the maximum excitation wavelength of CQDs is 370 nm, which is expressed as Ex = 370 nm. And it emits at 445 nm, indicating that the most suitable emission wavelength is 445 nm, which is expressed as Em = 445 nm in Fig. 3(a). The illustrations show CQDs in physical images of ultraviolet and fluorescent lamps, the pale-yellow solution under lamps and blue fluorescence under UV lamps.

In addition, the stability and salt-resistance properties of CQDs were studied. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the fluorescence intensity of the probe can be maintained for 30 days at least, while the fluorescence intensity remains basically unchanged at about 97%, indicating excellent stability. As shown in Fig. 3(d), with the increase of the concentration of NaCl from 0 to 1 M, the fluorescence intensity was not greatly affected, only fluctuated by about 5%, indicating that the CQDs have good salt-resistance properties and can be extended to the life of high salt solutions for testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the detection specificity of CQDs as a fluorescent sensor, added 10 kinds of metal ions (Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+) to the CQDs sensing system under the same conditions. As can be seen from Fig. 4 (a), the fluorescence when Fe3+ is added to CQDs is the strongest, indicating that CQDs can efficiently detect Fe3+ in an aqueous solution, the other 10 kinds of ions have little ability to quench CQDs, illustrated as physical maps taken under ultraviolet light, consistent with the test results. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm decreases gradually as the concentration of Fe3+ increases, indicating that Fe3+ can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity. There is a good linear relationship in the range of 1–10 μM (R2 = 0.9903) in Fig. 4(c), and 10–100 μM (R2 = 0.9859) in Fig. 4(d). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.21 μM. (LOD = 3σ/S, wherein the σ is the standard deviation of the CQDs sample (N = 20), S is the slope of the calibration curve)[52].

To study the single selectivity of CQDs, a variety of potential interfering ions, including Mg2+, Mn2+, Li+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, and Pb2+, were added to the CQDs solution. As shown in Fig. S2, in the system of CQDs + Fe3+, when other interfering ions are added, the fluorescence is still quenched, and the quenching ratio does not change greatly. Therefore, the prepared CQDs can be used as fluorescent probes for the single detection of Fe3+.

In addition, we compared the LOD and linear range of Fe3+ in this work with other methods reported in the previous literature in detail. Table S1 summarizes the sensing performances of different fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery of CQDs/Fe3+ by Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence recovery of 13 amino acids (Cys, Tar, Met, Lys, Ser, Pro, His, Tyr, La, Phe, Man, Trp, Glu) to CQDs/Fe3+ was studied in Fig. 5 (a), and it can be seen Cys can make CQDs/Fe3+ the highest fluorescence recovery rate, indicating that CQDs can efficiently detect Cys in water solutions. As shown in Fig. 5(b), as the concentration of Cys increases from 0 to 200 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs/Fe3+ around 445 nm gradually increases, indicating that adding Cys can effectively recover the fluorescence of CQDs/Fe3+. There is also a good linear relationship in the range of 3–10 μM (R2 = 0.9960) in Fig. 5(c), and 10–50 μM (R2 = 0.9978) in Fig. 5(d), the LOD is 1.10 μM. We compared the LOD and linear range of Cys in this work with other methods reported in the previous literature in detail. Table S2 summarizes the sensing performances of different fluorescent probes for Cys detection.

To verify the reproducibility of CQDs fluorescent probes, we performed a cycle test on them. As shown in Fig. S3, adding Fe3+ fluorescence quenching, and adding Cys fluorescence will be recovery, and then cycled through 5 rounds of testing, the probe has a better fluorescence quenching and recovery ability, applied to real-life can be reused many times.

To investigate the mechanism of CQDs detection Fe3+ quenching, changes in the UV–vis spectrum were studied. By adding Fe3+ to CQDs, Fe3+ and CQDs collide, Fe3+ has strong electron affinity, and there is an empty orbit, and the surface of CQDs has nitrogen, oxygen and other functional groups, there are lonely pairs of electrons, Fe3+ can be quickly placed with it, and then combine to produce non-luminescent complexes that cause fluorescence to die out. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the UV–vis spectra of CQDs changed significantly, with a significant redshift at peaks at 290 nm and an absorption peak of C = C bonds disappearing at 218 nm. These results suggest that the presence of Fe3+ affects the surface state of CQDs, which is due to static quenching that leads to fluorescence quenching of CQDs [36].

In addition, observational Fig. 6(b) can find that the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs overlap with the absorption peaks in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+, indicating that the fluorescence of CQDs is shielded by Fe3+, that is, the fluorescence of CQDs is quenched by Fe3+, which is caused by the Inner-filter effect (IFE) phenomenon. Therefore, it was concluded that the detection of Fe3+ quenching by CQDs was caused by static quenching and IFE [53]. A highly efficient IFE phenomenon indicated that the absorption band of the absorber had sufficient spectral overlap with the excitation or emission bands of the fluorophore. Understanding mechanisms can give us a more thorough understanding of things themselves.

When Cys is added, because the -SH has of sulfhydryl amino acids have a stronger binding capacity with Fe3+, when Cys appears in the solution, Fe3+ is combined with Cys first, allowing Fe3+ to be released from the CQDs surface, and finally the fluorescence on the CQDs surface is restored [32,54,55].

Since CQDs have good electrical conductivity, electrochemical methods are used for detection, and LOD results are mutually validated with fluorescence detection. When Cys was used as the test solution, the DPV curve obtained by CQDs modified glassy carbon electrode was a straight line without any change. Therefore, Cys with different concentrations were added into the FeCl3·6H2O solution to detect indirectly. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the effect of adding 300 μL of different concentrations of Cys to 10 mL 5 mM FeCl3·6H2O solution was studied, due to the combination of Cys and Fe3+, the peak current gradually decreases as different concentrations of Cys are added. And there is a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9961) in Fig. 7(b), and the LOD is calculated to be 1.13 μM, which is basically consistent with the LOD obtained by the fluorescence test. This suggests that CQDs detection Fe3+ can be mutually validated in two ways. In previous work, it was rare to combine the two channels, so our work was innovative and fully play the two characteristics of CQDs.

To verify the feasibility of the CQDs in detecting Fe3+ in real life, the actual sample detection using lake water and laboratory tap water was carried out. As can be seen from Table S3, the recovery rate of Fe3+ in lake water and tap water of the laboratory is between 99.04 and 107.85%, and RSD (n = 3) is between 0.81 and 3.25%. These results show that despite the presence many of mineral and biological compounds in the water sample, CQDs can effectively distinguish Fe3+ from other compounds, which is a good demonstration of the feasibility and reliability of CQDs for quantitative detection of Fe3+ in real samples.

The potential application of the CQDs/Fe3+ sensing platform for detecting Cys in human urine samples. As can be seen from Table S3 , the recovery rate varied from 96.70% to 101.95%, and RSD is between 0.76 and 3.05%. These results revealed the reliability of the CQDs/Fe3+ based sensing platform for the determination of Cys in human urine samples.

Likewise, electrochemical methods are also used to detect real samples. As can be seen from Table S4 , the recovery rate of Fe3+ in lake water and tap water of the laboratory is between 97.58 and 103.01%, and RSD (n = 3) is between 0.93 and 3.08%. The recovery rate of Cys in human urine samples varied from 97.55% to 101.53%, and RSD is between 0.88 and 2.79%. It is basically consistent with the fluorescence detection results. The results show that both fluorescence and electrochemical methods can be applied to real-life detection, expanding their applications and giving them new life.

CQDs are used as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks due to their excellent fluorescence property. Use the purchased stamp to imprint the pattern on non-fluorescent white paper, the unprocessed image is not fluorescent under fluorescent light in Fig. S4(a). Immerse the purchased stamp in the prepared CQDs solution without any treatment, and then imprint the soaked seal on white paper. Now the image processed with CQDs fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink is fluorescent under fluorescent light in Fig. S4(b), so it can be extended to the application of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink, and the operation is simple and does not require secondary processing. It can also be used as a fluorescent ink for writing numbers and drawings [37]. The process is very simple and can be completed quickly without technical guidance. It can be extended to anti-counterfeiting of information without high cost and is very simple. Thus, the CQDs can be a potential candidate for anticounterfeiting and data encryption.

In summary, we successfully prepared blue fluorescence CQDs which can quickly identify Fe3+ and Cys. CQDs maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 370 and 445 nm. Adding Fe3+ fluorescence quenching, and adding Cys fluorescence will be recovery. LOD of Fe3+ was 1.21 μM and LOD of Cys was 1.10 μM. Then, DPV detection was performed using electrochemistry, and the LOD was calculated to be 1.13 μM. It shows that the prepared CQDs can be detected in both fluorescence and electrochemical methods, and the calculated LOD is basically the same. With its low raw material price, simple synthetic CQDs process, and can be simple, efficient, and fast detection of Fe3+ and Cys, it is expected to be widely used in drinking water testing, drug testing and biomedical aspects. At the same time, it can be used as fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting of information.

Rui Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Hebing Pei: Conceptualization. Simin He: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Hui Zheng: Data curation. Wanjing Liu: Supervision, Investigation. Xuening Jin: Supervision, Methodology. Xuejiao Zhang: Supervision. Nijuan Liu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Software, Writing - review & editing. Ruibin Guo: Supervision. Zunli Mo: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51262027, 21861034); the Science and Technology Project of Gansu Province, China (20YF3GA022); the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (18JR3RA094).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114118.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots applied to methodology on detection of pesticide and veterinary drug residues
J. Agric. Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou J W, Zou X M, Song S H, et al. Quantum Dots Applied to Methodology on Detection of Pesticide and Veterinary Drug Residues. J Agric Food Chem, 2018, 66(6): 1307-1319.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modification-free carbon dots as turn-on fluorescence probe for detection of organophosphorus pesticides
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lin B, Yan Y, Guo M, et al. Modification-free carbon dots as turn-on fluorescence probe for detection of organophosphorus pesticides. Food Chem, 2018, 245: 1176-1182.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of carbon dots and their application in food analysis as signal probe
Chin. J. Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ye S-L, Huang J-J, Luo L, et al. Preparation of Carbon Dots and Their Application in Food Analysis as Signal Probe. Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(10): 1571-1581.

Adv. Funct. Mater.
